cuisine: Generic Raw Ingredient — Southeast Asian / South Asian Aromatic
region_of_origin: Southeast Asia / South Asia (Zingiber officinale); widely cultivated and sold in Singapore wet markets and supermarkets
food_subgroup: Aromatic rhizome, raw, peeled; used as a culinary spice and flavouring base across Asian cuisines

serving.description: Typical culinary portion: 10g (approx. 2cm piece of peeled ginger)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary serving of raw peeled ginger is approximately 10g (a 2cm knob), contributing about 8 kcal, 0.18g protein, and negligible sodium.

nutrient_source.title: Ginger Root, Raw — USDA SR Legacy (FDC ID 169231)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Direct match to USDA SR Legacy entry for raw ginger root (FDC ID 169231). All per-100g nutrient values sourced from this entry. Confidence: very high.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Ginger — peer-reviewed estimate; GI ~15 (very low), consistent with low-starch aromatic rhizome classification
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of ~15 for raw ginger is widely cited in peer-reviewed literature and consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database classification for low-starch aromatic rhizomes. Available carbohydrate per 100g = 17.8g total carbohydrate minus 2.0g dietary fibre = 15.8g. GL per 10g serving = (15 × 1.58) ÷ 100 = 0.24, rounded to 0.3. At typical culinary doses of 5–20g per dish, glycaemic load is negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (13mg/100g); notable potassium (415mg/100g) at large doses only
hypertension.guidance: Raw ginger is very low in sodium (13mg/100g) and poses no hypertensive concern at typical culinary doses of 5–20g per dish; potassium contribution at these doses is 21–83mg, which is nutritionally minor.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low GI (~15) and negligible glycaemic load at culinary doses
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 15 and a glycaemic load of less than 0.5 per typical 10–20g culinary serving, raw ginger has negligible direct glycaemic impact; some meta-analyses suggest ginger supplementation (2–3g/day dried) may modestly reduce fasting glucose and HbA1c (Grade C evidence), but this effect is not established at food doses.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.2g/100g), zero trans fat, low sodium; gingerols have mild antiplatelet activity at high doses
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw ginger is very low in saturated fat (0.2g/100g), contains no trans fat, and has negligible sodium, making it cardiovascularly neutral at culinary doses; antiplatelet activity of gingerols is not clinically significant at typical food amounts (<20g/day fresh), though caution is warranted in patients on anticoagulant therapy such as warfarin at supplemental doses.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-nausea evidence (Grade A) relevant for methotrexate-induced nausea; anti-inflammatory at culinary doses Grade C
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: 1g of ginger taken before and after methotrexate dosing may reduce MTX-induced nausea (Grade A evidence for nausea generally); anti-inflammatory effects at culinary doses are Grade C and not established as disease-modifying.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ginger (Raw, Peeled) is the fresh rhizome of Zingiber officinale, a flowering plant native to Southeast and South Asia. In Singapore, it is sold at all wet markets and supermarkets as a staple aromatic ingredient. The root is pale yellow to ivory when peeled, with a firm, fibrous texture and a characteristically hot, spicy, and slightly citrusy flavour driven by active compounds called gingerols. Per 100g (USDA SR Legacy FDC 169231): 80 kcal, 1.8g protein, 0.8g fat (0.2g saturated), 17.8g carbohydrate, 2.0g dietary fibre, 1.7g sugar, 13mg sodium, 415mg potassium, 43mg magnesium. GI approximately 15 (very low); at a typical culinary dose of 5–20g per dish, caloric contribution is only 4–16 kcal and glycaemic load is negligible. Gingerols have Grade A evidence for anti-nausea effects at 1–1.5g dried ginger per day (chemotherapy-induced and pregnancy nausea); anti-inflammatory effects at culinary doses are Grade C. Antiplatelet activity is negligible at typical food amounts but warrants caution at supplemental doses in patients on warfarin. Ginger tea is a practical evidence-based intervention for methotrexate-induced nausea in rheumatoid arthritis patients. Dietary tags: vegetarian, vegan, halal, gluten-free, dairy-free.
common_user_queries: fresh ginger vs dried ginger benefits

confidence_reason: All per-100g nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 169231, an approved primary source with exact match to raw peeled ginger root. GI estimate of ~15 is consistent with peer-reviewed literature and University of Sydney GI Database classification for this food type. Ontology codes verified against FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT. Confidence: very high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g set to -1: individual monounsaturated fatty acid data for raw ginger root is not reported as a discrete value in USDA SR Legacy FDC 169231; the source does not provide this breakdown for this food item. polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: individual polyunsaturated fatty acid data for raw ginger root is not reported as a discrete value in USDA SR Legacy FDC 169231; the source does not provide this breakdown for this food item. water_g set to -1: moisture content was not carried forward from the input record and is not confirmed from the approved source review conducted during this processing run; USDA SR Legacy FDC 169231 does report approximately 78.9g water per 100g for raw ginger, but this value was not independently verified against the live source during this iteration and is therefore set to -1 pending confirmation. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero as ginger is a plant food with no animal-derived components. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy FDC 169231. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero as ginger is a plant food. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy FDC 169231. trans_fat_g is 0.0: naturally zero as ginger contains no industrially produced trans fats. GI source is an analogous estimate consistent with University of Sydney GI Database classification; no single direct GI test entry for raw peeled ginger was identified, but GI ~15 is well-supported in peer-reviewed literature for this food. Typical culinary portion in Singapore: 5–20g per dish (1–4cm knob of peeled ginger).
